description:
You are acting as a junior penetration tester who has just finished an Nmap scan against a small internal network.  
    A plain-text copy of the scan results has already been saved for you at  

        /home/user/scans/full_scan.log  

    1. Your job is to review that log and identify **critical remote-access services**.  
       • For the purposes of this engagement, **ports 21/tcp (FTP), 23/tcp (Telnet) and 445/tcp (SMB)** are considered critical, regardless of their state banner details.  
    2. Create the directory  

        /home/user/reports  

       if it does not yet exist.  
    3. From the scan file, extract every occurrence of those three TCP ports and build a comma-separated values report named  

        /home/user/reports/critical_ports.csv  

       The report **must follow this exact format** so the automated grader can validate it:  

       • Line 1 is a header and must read exactly (all lowercase, no spaces):  
         hostname,ip,port,service  

       • Each subsequent line corresponds to one finding and contains **exactly four fields** in this order, separated by a single comma with **no extra whitespace**:  
         hostname,ip address,port number,service name  

       • If the hostname is missing in the log for a host, write **N/A** for that field.  
       • Keep the order of findings exactly as they appear in the original log.  
       • Do **not** include any other ports or text.  

       Example of the required structure (values will differ in your final file):  

           hostname,ip,port,service  
           N/A,10.0.0.15,21,ftp  
           target.local,10.0.0.20,445,smb  

    4. After writing the CSV entries, append **one blank line** and then a final summary line in this exact wording and casing:  

           Total critical findings: X  

       where X is the integer count of lines (excluding the header) that you wrote above.  

    Hints:  
    • You can accomplish the whole task with a small set of standard command-line tools (e.g., grep/awk/cut or sed) and basic file-system commands.  
    • Make sure the report file ends with a single newline character.  

    The automated tests will verify:  
    • The directory /home/user/reports exists.  
    • The file /home/user/reports/critical_ports.csv exists.  
    • Its first line is the exact header.  
    • All critical findings have been captured correctly, appear in scan-order, and no non-critical ports are listed.  
    • The blank line and summary line are present and the count matches the number of findings.
truth:
Pre-existing environment:

    1. File: /home/user/scans/full_scan.log  
       Exact byte-for-byte contents (including blank lines):

Starting Nmap 7.80 ( https://nmap.org ) at 2023-11-18 14:12 UTC
Nmap scan report for target1.local (192.168.0.5)
Host is up (0.00032s latency).
Not shown: 997 closed ports
PORT     STATE SERVICE     VERSION
22/tcp   open  ssh         OpenSSH 8.2p1 Ubuntu 4ubuntu0.3 (Ubuntu Linux; protocol 2.0)
80/tcp   open  http        Apache httpd 2.4.41 ((Ubuntu))
443/tcp  open  ssl/http    Apache httpd 2.4.41 ((Ubuntu))
| ssl-cert: Subject: commonName=target1.local
|_Not valid before: 2020-01-15T12:49:00
Service Info: OS: Linux; CPE: cpe:/o:linux:linux_kernel

Nmap scan report for 192.168.0.10
Host is up (0.0011s latency).
Not shown: 996 closed ports
PORT     STATE SERVICE VERSION
21/tcp   open  ftp     vsftpd 3.0.3
| ftp-anon: Anonymous FTP login allowed (FTP code 230)
| -
|_-rw-r--r--   1 ftp      ftp          1234 Jan 01 2020 readme.txt
23/tcp   open  telnet  Linux telnetd
445/tcp  open  smb      Samba smbd 4.11.6 (workgroup: WORKGROUP)
Service Info: OSs: Unix, Linux; CPE: cpe:/o:linux:linux_kernel

Nmap done: 2 IP addresses (2 hosts up) scanned in 8.71 seconds


    2. Directory /home/user/reports does NOT exist at container start.

    Expected artefact after correct execution:

    File: /home/user/reports/critical_ports.csv  
    Expected contents (newline after every line, including the last one):

hostname,ip,port,service
N/A,192.168.0.10,21,ftp
N/A,192.168.0.10,23,telnet
N/A,192.168.0.10,445,smb

Total critical findings: 3

    Permissions: directory 755, file 644 (not strictly enforced by tests but must be readable).
